steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank fills the gap between two independent clauses: “Dickens's novel originally had a different [blank]” and “while writing and editing, Dickens had planned to call the novel Mag's Diversions.” The word “though” is a conjunctive adverb that must be placed correctly, and the two independent clauses must be joined with appropriate punctuation.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'title, though;': The comma after “title” sets off “though” as a trailing conjunctive adverb closing the first clause; the semicolon then correctly joins the two independent clauses. This is the only structure that handles both the conjunctive adverb and the clause boundary properly. ✓
  • Choice B — 'title, though,': A comma after “though” leaves the second independent clause joined to the first by only a comma — a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice C — 'title; though': The semicolon comes before “though,” making “though” introduce the second clause as a subordinating conjunction. But “while writing and editing, Dickens had planned...” is an independent clause, and “though” used this way would make it a dependent clause fragment. Also, a semicolon before a subordinating conjunction is not standard. ✗
  • Choice D — 'title, though': No punctuation after “though” runs the second independent clause directly into the first with only a comma separating the full constructions — another comma splice. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
